summary: 10 concrete hardware, AI, and developer-tool items surfaced from 25 scraped stories.

items.name: OpenWrt One
items.summary: OpenWrt One is an open hardware router with community-backed firmware.
items.why_included: The thread had hands-on praise and practical upgrade talk, not just announcement churn.
items.evidence: A commenter said they had just received one because most routers feel low quality, while others discussed OpenWrt Two, GL.iNet, and mesh upgrades.

items.name: AMD Ryzen AI Halo – $4k AI Dev Kit
items.source_story_title: AMD Ryzen AI Halo – $4k AI Dev Kit
items.summary: A $4k Strix Halo dev kit aimed at local AI workloads.
items.why_included: Commenters treated it as a real step in AMD’s AI hardware push, not a toy.
items.evidence: Replies called AMD’s Playbooks a serious response to Nvidia Spark and compared the kit with Framework Desktop and other Strix Halo builds.

items.name: Real-time map of Great Britain's rail network
items.source_story_title: Real-time map of Great Britain's rail network
items.summary: Signalbox’s live rail map shows trains moving across Great Britain.
items.why_included: Commenters described it as genuinely useful, not just a neat visualization.
items.evidence: One commenter said they regularly use it to watch trains and found it pretty good even if the projections are imperfect.

items.name: Pulpie
items.source_story_title: Show HN: Pulpie – Models for Cleaning the Web
items.summary: Pulpie is a model-driven web cleaner that preserves main content, tables, and images.
items.why_included: The thread confirmed it does useful extraction work and compares favorably with heuristic cleaners.
items.evidence: The author said images and tables pass through, and that the model outperformed heuristics by 25 F1 points on their tests.

items.name: A global workspace in language models
items.source_story_title: A global workspace in language models
items.summary: Anthropic’s research explores a global-workspace style internal architecture for LLMs.
items.why_included: The comments dug into the model-internal details rather than treating it as generic research fluff.
items.evidence: Readers quoted the paper’s internal representation findings and asked for more detail about how the model was trained.

items.name: OfficeCLI
items.source_story_title: OfficeCLI: Office suite for AI agents to read and edit Microsoft Office files
items.summary: OfficeCLI lets AI agents read and edit Microsoft Office files.
items.why_included: The discussion surfaced compliance details and real alternatives, which made it feel concrete.
items.evidence: Commenters compared it with ECMA-376-compliant projects and a related Smalldocs office suite for agents and humans.

items.name: Road to Elm 1.0
items.summary: Elm’s faster-builds work keeps the language moving toward 1.0.
items.why_included: Commenters still treat Elm as a focused language with long-lived influence.
items.evidence: Replies called Elm an influential, highly focused language and discussed its lack of a public roadmap alongside the build-speed work.

items.name: Clojure 1.13 adds support for checked keys
items.source_story_title: Clojure 1.13 adds support for checked keys
items.summary: Clojure 1.13 adds checked keys for clearer map contracts.
items.why_included: The feature prompted practical discussion about safer data boundaries and ergonomics.
items.evidence: Replies focused on required versus optional checked keys, runtime guarantees, and how they fit with spec’ed maps.

items.name: Kani: A Model Checker for Rust
items.source_story_title: Kani: A Model Checker for Rust
items.summary: Kani is a Rust model checker aimed at verifying program behavior.
items.why_included: The thread pointed to a useful tutorial and positioned Kani against other property-testing tools.
items.evidence: Commenters linked the tutorial and the older paper, and compared Kani to hypothesis-auto-style tooling.

items.name: Linux on the Atari Jaguar. No, really.
items.source_story_title: Linux on the Atari Jaguar. No, really.
items.summary: A Linux port that runs on original Atari Jaguar hardware and reaches a BusyBox shell.
items.why_included: The repo is real and the comments emphasize how little special hardware it needs.
items.evidence: Readers noted it works on stock hardware, uses only 2 MB of RAM, and gets to a BusyBox shell without flash carts.

telegram_footer: 10 items surfaced | 25/25 stories scraped | history + run artifact updated
